title: At the Bakery
summary: A woman visits a bakery and asks about bread and pastries. She talks with the baker about prices and makes a purchase.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Good morning! Do you have any fresh bread today?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Good morning! Yes, we have white bread and brown bread.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: How much is the white bread?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: It is two pounds for a small loaf.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: The brown bread is two pounds fifty.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: I will take one white loaf, please.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Of course. Would you like anything else?

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: Yes, do you have any croissants?

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: Yes, we have plain croissants and chocolate croissants.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: They are one pound twenty each.

body.speaker: Customer
body.text: I will take two plain croissants, please.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: That is four pounds forty in total.

body.speaker: Baker
body.text: And here is your sixty pence change. Have a nice day!

annotations.exampleEn: I bought fresh bread from the bakery this morning.
She bought a loaf of bread and some butter.

annotations.meaningVi: đồng bảng Anh (đơn vị tiền tệ)

annotations.exampleEn: He had a croissant and a cup of coffee for breakfast.
I prefer a plain croissant without any filling.
That is ten pounds in total.
She gave me fifty pence change.

annotations.meaningVi: xu (tiền lẻ Anh, số nhiều của 'penny')
annotations.exampleEn: A chocolate bar costs seventy pence.

annotations.meaningVi: Bạn có muốn thêm gì không?
annotations.exampleEn: After taking her order, the waiter asked, 'Would you like anything else?'

annotations.meaningVi: tôi sẽ lấy / tôi muốn mua
annotations.exampleEn: I will take the brown loaf, please.
The shopkeeper smiled and said, 'Have a nice day!'

grammarNotes.title: Asking about prices with 'How much'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để hỏi giá cả, dùng 'How much is...?' (số ít) hoặc 'How much are...?' (số nhiều). Ví dụ: 'How much is the white bread?' / 'How much are they?' Đây là cấu trúc rất thực dụng khi đi mua sắm.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To ask about the price of something, use 'How much is...?' for singular items and 'How much are...?' for plural items. For example: 'How much is the white bread?' / 'How much are they?' This is essential language for shopping.

grammarNotes.title: Making polite requests with 'please'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Thêm 'please' vào cuối câu yêu cầu để lịch sự hơn: 'I will take one white loaf, please.' Hoặc dùng 'Could I have...?' / 'Can I have...?' Trong giao tiếp hằng ngày, 'please' là từ quan trọng để thể hiện sự lịch sự.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Adding 'please' to the end of a request makes it polite: 'I will take one white loaf, please.' You can also say 'Could I have...?' or 'Can I have...?'. Using 'please' is an important part of polite everyday communication.

translationVi:
Khách: Chào buổi sáng! Hôm nay bạn có bánh mì tươi không?
Thợ làm bánh: Chào buổi sáng! Có, chúng tôi có bánh mì trắng và bánh mì đen.
Khách: Bánh mì trắng giá bao nhiêu?
Thợ làm bánh: Một ổ nhỏ giá hai bảng.
Khách: Còn bánh mì đen thì sao?
Thợ làm bánh: Bánh mì đen giá hai bảng rưỡi.
Khách: Cho tôi một ổ bánh mì trắng.
Thợ làm bánh: Được thôi. Bạn có muốn thêm gì không?
Khách: Có, bạn có bánh sừng bò không?
Thợ làm bánh: Có, chúng tôi có bánh sừng bò trơn và bánh sừng bò sô-cô-la.
Khách: Chúng giá bao nhiêu?
Thợ làm bánh: Mỗi cái giá một bảng hai mươi xu.
Khách: Cho tôi hai cái bánh sừng bò trơn.
Thợ làm bánh: Tổng cộng là bốn bảng bốn mươi xu.
Khách: Đây là năm bảng.
Thợ làm bánh: Và đây là tiền thối sáu mươi xu của bạn. Chúc một ngày tốt lành!
Khách: Cảm ơn. Bạn cũng vậy nhé!

questions.prompt: What two kinds of bread does the bakery have?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'we have white bread and brown bread.'
questions.hint: Listen to what the baker says about bread types.

questions.prompt: How much does the white bread cost?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'It is two pounds for a small loaf.'
questions.hint: What price does the baker give for white bread?

questions.prompt: How much does the brown bread cost?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'The brown bread is two pounds fifty.'
questions.hint: Is the brown bread cheaper or more expensive than white bread?

questions.prompt: What does the customer buy first?
questions.explanation: The customer says 'I will take one white loaf, please.'
questions.hint: What bread does the customer choose?

questions.prompt: What two types of croissants does the bakery sell?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'we have plain croissants and chocolate croissants.'
questions.hint: One type is plain. What is the other?

questions.prompt: How much does each croissant cost?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'They are one pound twenty each.'
questions.hint: What is the price per croissant?

questions.prompt: How many croissants does the customer buy?
questions.explanation: The customer says 'I will take two plain croissants, please.'
questions.hint: How many does she say she wants?

questions.prompt: What is the total cost of the customer's order?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'That is four pounds forty in total.'
questions.hint: What total does the baker announce?

questions.prompt: How much money does the customer give the baker?
questions.explanation: The customer says 'Here is five pounds.'
questions.hint: What note does the customer hand over?

questions.prompt: How much change does the customer receive?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'here is your sixty pence change.'
questions.hint: What is the difference between five pounds and four pounds forty?

questions.prompt: What does the baker say at the end of the conversation?
questions.explanation: The baker says 'Have a nice day!' as a friendly farewell.
questions.hint: What polite wish does the baker give at the end?

cloze.template: Customer: Good morning! Do you have any fresh {{b1}} today? Baker: Good morning! Yes, we have white bread and brown bread. Customer: How much is the white bread? Baker: It is two {{b2}} for a small loaf. Customer: And the brown bread? Baker: The brown bread is two pounds {{b3}}. Customer: I will take one white loaf, please. Baker: Of course. Would you like anything {{b4}}? Customer: Yes, do you have any {{b5}}? Baker: Yes, we have plain croissants and chocolate croissants. Customer: How much are they? Baker: They are one pound twenty {{b6}}. Customer: I will take two {{b7}} croissants, please. Baker: That is four pounds forty in {{b8}}. Customer: Here is five pounds. Baker: And here is your sixty pence {{b9}}. Have a nice {{b10}}! Customer: Thank you. You too!

blanks.explanation: The customer asks about 'fresh bread' — the main product at a bakery.
'Two pounds' is the price. Pounds (£) is British currency.
The brown bread costs 'two pounds fifty' — fifty pence more than the white bread.
'Would you like anything else?' is a standard phrase when serving a customer.
The customer asks for 'croissants' — a pastry mentioned throughout the dialogue.
'One pound twenty each' means the price is per item.
The customer chooses 'plain' croissants, not chocolate ones.
'In total' means the combined price of everything bought.
'Change' is the money returned when you pay more than the price.
'Have a nice day' is a common friendly goodbye in English-speaking countries.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The customer buys white bread instead of brown bread. Do you think the price difference is a good reason to choose one food over another? What other things might people think about when buying food?
